item.title: GCP Workload Identity Federation
item.author: Mohamed Rasvi
item.content:encoded: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) offers a robust set of tools, but some of its most powerful features remain underutilized due to lackluster marketing and sparse documentation. Workload Identity Federation (WIF) is one such gem, enabling secure, keyless authentication for external systems like GitHub Actions to access GCP APIs without the risks of long-lived credentials like service account keys. By directly mapping external identities to GCP IAM roles, WIF eliminates the need for intermediary service accounts, streamlining security for CI/CD pipelines. However, its complexity and limited guidance have kept it obscure. This article explores WIF’s untapped potential, critiques GCP’s failure to promote it, and provides a step-by-step guide to implement it within a GCP organization for secure GitHub Actions deployments.The Problem: GCP’s Missed Opportunity with WIFWorkload Identity Federation is a stellar security tool, yet it’s barely a blip on GCP’s marketing radar. Unlike flagship services like BigQuery or Cloud Run, WIF lacks the visibility it deserves. GCP’s documentation, while technically detailed, is dense and assumes familiarity with OpenID Connect (OIDC) and IAM, leaving the average developer let’s call them “Joe” struggling to get started. Practical tutorials are scarce, and the setup process feels like navigating a maze.This oversight is costly. WIF’s ability to directly authenticate external identities to GCP resources without service account keys addresses a critical security pain point: credential management. By not promoting WIF or providing clear, beginner-friendly guides, GCP has let a killer product languish in obscurity. Compare this to AWS’s OIDC integration for GitHub Actions, which enjoys better documentation and community traction, and it’s clear GCP needs to rethink its approach.Why Workload Identity Federation Shines for SecurityTraditional GCP authentication for CI/CD pipelines often involves service account keys — JSON files with static credentials that are prone to leaks if mismanaged. Workload Identity Federation bypasses this by using to directly map external identities (e.g., GitHub Actions) to GCP IAM principals. This approach is a security game-changer for several reasons:: WIF uses ephemeral tokens (valid for ~5 minutes), drastically reducing the risk of credential exposure.: External identities can be granted IAM roles directly, eliminating the need for service accounts and simplifying permission management.: Permissions are scoped to specific resources and repositories, ensuring least-privilege access.: WIF integrates seamlessly with CI/CD pipelines, supporting deployments across multiple GCP projects.For organizations building secure CI/CD pipelines, WIF’s keyless, direct authentication is a must-have. Yet, its steep learning curve and lack of clear documentation have kept it out of the mainstream.Implementing WIF in a GCP Organization StructureTo showcase WIF’s power, let’s walk through setting it up within a GCP organization for a CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions. We’ll focus on direct authentication, granting GitHub identities IAM roles without service accounts.Step 1: Define the GCP Organization StructureGCP organizes resources hierarchically: an contains , which house . For a CI/CD setup:: Use Cloud Identity or Google Workspace to create a GCP Organization, the root node for resource management.: Organize projects logically. For example, a “CI/CD” folder for pipeline resources and a “Production” folder for live applications.: Under the CI/CD folder, create a project (e.g., cicd-pipeline) for pipeline configuration. Add projects like app-prod and app-dev under other folders for application deployments.This structure enables centralized IAM policies at the organization or folder level, with projects inheriting permissions.Step 2: Configure Workload Identity FederationWIF uses a and to establish trust between GCP and GitHub Actions. Here’s how to set it up for direct authentication:Create a Workload Identity Pool:In the GCP Console, go to IAM & Admin > Workload Identity Federation.Create a pool named github-cicd-pool.Or use the gcloud CLI for automation:gcloud iam workload-identity-pools create github-cicd-pool \ --location="global" \ --display-name="GitHub CI/CD Pool"Add a GitHub OIDC Provider:gcloud iam workload-identity-pools providers create-oidc github-provider \ --location="global" \ --workload-identity-pool="github-cicd-pool" \ --issuer-uri="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com/" \ --attribute-mapping="google.subject=assertion.sub,attribute.repository=assertion.repository" \ --attribute-condition="assertion.repository=='your-org/your-repo'"Note the provider’s full path: projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/providers/github-provider:Instead of linking a service account, bind IAM roles directly to the workload identity pool principal.For example, grant roles/storage.admin to the GitHub repository’s identity for Cloud Storage access in the app-prod project:gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding app-prod \ --member="principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/attribute.repository/your-org/your-repo" \ --role="roles/storage.admin"Replace YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER with the numeric ID of the cicd-pipeline project hosting the identity pool.Step 3: Set Up GitHub Actions for Direct AuthenticationConfigure your GitHub Actions workflow to authenticate to GCP using WIF’s OIDC tokens::Use the google-github-actions/auth action to exchange GitHub’s OIDC token for GCP access.Example workflow to deploy files to Cloud Storage:name: Deploy to GCPon: branches: [ main ] deploy: permissions: id-token: 'write' - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2 with: workload_identity_provider: 'projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/providers/github-provider' - run: gcloud storage cp ./my-app/* gs://my-bucket/Push a change to the main branch to trigger the workflow.GitHub Actions generates an OIDC token, which GCP validates against the workload identity provider, granting access to the specified resources.Step 4: Scale Across ProjectsTo deploy to multiple projects (e.g., app-prod, app-dev):Add IAM policy bindings for the workload identity principal in each target project, granting roles like roles/compute.admin for Compute Engine.Update the GitHub Actions workflow to target different projects using the project_id parameter or environment variables:- run: gcloud storage cp ./my-app/* gs://my-bucket/ --project=app-prodUse folder-level IAM policies to apply permissions across multiple projects, ensuring consistent access for the CI/CD pipeline.Challenges and LimitationsImplementing WIF without service accounts is powerful but not without hurdles:: Configuring attribute mappings and direct IAM bindings requires understanding OIDC and GCP IAM, which can intimidate newcomers.: GitHub’s OIDC tokens expire in ~5 minutes, requiring workflows to execute quickly or implement token refresh logic.: GCP’s guides rarely cover direct principal-based WIF, and examples for GitHub Actions are even harder to find.: Direct IAM bindings can become unwieldy for large organizations, requiring careful role assignments to avoid over-privileging.These challenges highlight the need for better documentation and tools to simplify WIF adoption.To boost WIF’s adoption, GCP should:: Offer clear, step-by-step guides for direct WIF authentication, including GitHub Actions and other CI/CD platforms.: Feature WIF in security and DevOps campaigns, emphasizing its keyless, direct authentication benefits.: Develop CLI tools or Console wizards to automate identity pool setup and IAM bindings.: Encourage blogs, videos, and tutorials to showcase WIF’s real-world applications.

item.title: Ambatuscrum #9: Monitorama: Taming Your Django Dragons
item.author: Soros F
item.content:encoded: Modern web applications demand more than just functionality — they must be reliable, fast, and transparent when things go wrong. In this post, you’ll learn how to architect a monitoring stack that covers:Built‑in platform monitoring (Firebase, GCP, AWS, DigitalOcean) for error, log, user‑activity, and performance monitoring (Sentry, ELK/Cloud Logging, Google Analytics, New Relic) for your own business logic (e.g. tracking critical function calls): dashboards, alerts, and continuous improvementWe’ll illustrate with a real‑world example: instrumenting a Django REST Framework (DRF) service running in Docker, using Prometheus and Grafana.1. Why Monitoring MattersImagine a customer filling out a form on your site — then suddenly nothing happens. Did the server crash? Was there a network spike? Without proper monitoring, you’re blind. With it, you can: incidents in real time root causes by correlating errors, logs, and performance metrics resource usage and user experience the team before SLAs are breached2. Leverage Built‑In Platform MonitoringMost cloud and mobile platforms ship with free monitoring “for free”: Activate these out of the box. Even if you plan to deploy third‑party tools later, you’ll have a baseline.3. Standard Monitoring StackA healthy observability stack typically includes four pillars:3.1 Error Monitoring with Sentry: Install the Sentry SDK in Django (sentry-sdk[django]), configure DSN. to capture real client IP in Sentry# myapp/middleware.pyfrom ipware import get_client_ipclass ClientIPMiddleware: def __init__(self, get_response): self.get_response = get_response def __call__(self, request): client_ip, _ = get_client_ip(request) real_port = request.META.get("HTTP_X_REAL_PORT") # attach “ip_address” field to every Sentry event "ip_address": real_port and f"{client_ip}:{real_port}" }) return self.get_response(request)# settings.pyimport sentry_sdkfrom sentry_sdk.integrations.django import DjangoIntegration dsn=os.environ["SENTRY_DSN"], integrations=[DjangoIntegration()], traces_sample_rate=0.2, # adjust for performance tracing send_default_pii=True, # include user context from middleware) # … "myapp.middleware.ClientIPMiddleware", # …: Every unhandled exception (and breadcrumbs leading up to it) appears in Sentry with user context.This view combines APM (transactions) and query‑level performance data collected by the Sentry Python SDK.A. Most Time‑Consuming Queries: SQL statements ranked by total time spent executing.: Reveals hotspots in your ORM or raw SQL that slow responses (e.g. heavy INSERT INTO silk_request … or slow UPDATE statements).: Add indexes, batch writes, or reduce fields logged if these queries are not business‑critical.B. Most Time‑Consuming Domains: External HTTP calls (by domain) ranked by total latency.: Third‑party API calls (e.g. to *.ui.ac.id:8080) can dominate your request time.: Cache responses, add retries with backoff, or move calls into background jobs.C. Transactions Per Minute: Number of traced transactions (i.e. instrumented Django views/endpoints) per minute.: Correlate throughput with error spikes or latency changes.: If TPS jumps but p95 latency stays flat, your autoscaling is healthy; if latency also jumps, investigate resource constraints.: The 50th‑ and 75th‑percentile durations of your transactions over the selected time window.: p50 shows typical user experience; p75 begins to expose the “slower quartile.”: Set alert thresholds on p75 or p95 to catch regressions before they impact most users.: Ship your Django logs (JSON‑formatted) to Elasticsearch or Grafana Loki.: You can query “which endpoints log ERROR most often?” and pivot on request parameters.3.3 User Activity (Google Analytics): Add the GA4 snippet to your frontend (or server‑side track events via Measurement Protocol).: Understand user flows, drop‑off points, and conversion funnels.3.4 Platform Performance (New Relic): Install the New Relic agent in your Python process, set your license key.: Get out‑of‑the‑box APM dashboards showing throughput, transaction traces, database query times.4. Case Study: Prometheus & Grafana on Dockerized DRFHere’s how I wired up Prometheus + Grafana to my Django REST Framework app in Docker. Within minutes I had real‑time metrics flowing.4.1 Docker‑Compose Overview$ docker ps -aCONTAINER ID IMAGE STATUS PORTSf50d645d10eb myapp-be-staging:latest Up 37h 8001→800027a244b363cd prom/prometheus Up 7h 9090→9090b3bce01bde5a grafana/grafana Up 6d 3002→3000…4.2 Prometheus Configuration (prometheus.yml)global: scrape_interval: 15s - job_name: 'prometheus' - targets: ['localhost:9090']- job_name: 'singawas-be-production' metrics_path: /metrics/ - targets: ['myapp-be-production:8000'] - job_name: 'singawas-be-dev' metrics_path: /metrics/ - targets: ['myapp-be-staging:8000'] - job_name: 'singawas-fe' static_configs: - targets: ['myapp:3000']remote_write: - url: https://your-remote-write-endpoint basic_auth: password: ***Two back‑end jobs (production & staging) scraping /metrics/ from DRF’s Prometheus exporterFront‑end jobs to track overall request counts (if instrumented)Remote write to a central metrics store4.3 Grafana Dashboard HighlightsBelow are the panels from your “Simple Django” and “Django” dashboards, with the PromQL (or concept) behind each and why it matters.: total HTTP requests per second (or minute) across all endpointssum(rate(http_requests_total[1m])): baseline throughput — spikes may indicate heavy load or abuse.2. 2XX / 4XX / 5XX Responses: break‑out of successful vs. client‑error vs. server‑error ratessum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"2.."}[1m])) sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"4.."}[1m])) sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m])): rising 4XX might signal bad client calls; rising 5XX is a server‑failure alert.3. Request Latency (p50, p95, p99): distribution of request durationshistogram_quantile(0.50, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) histogram_quantile(0.95, …) histogram_quantile(0.99, …): p95/p99 show your “tail latency” — critical for SLAs and user experience.: CPU seconds consumed per second by your app containersrate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container="myapp-be-staging"}[1m]): sudden jumps can indicate inefficient code paths or traffic storms.: memory footprint of your Django processcontainer_memory_working_set_bytes{container="myapp-be-staging"}: creeping memory use may herald a leak.: request count per route (e.g. /api/v1/orders/)sum(rate(http_requests_total[1m])) by (handler): identifies your hottest endpoints; focus optimization where it counts.: whether Prometheus can scrape each target (1 = up, 0 = down)up{job="singawas-be-production"}: instant red/green health indicator.8. Transactions Per Minute (Backend insights): total “transactions” (Sentry APM spans) per minute: correlate to request load and error spikes.9. p50 / p75 Duration (Backend insights): median and 75th‑percentile transaction duration from Sentry APM: complements Prometheus histograms with end‑to‑end tracing data.5. Advanced Usage: Alerts & Custom Dashboards5.1 Alerting with Prometheusgroups:- name: app-alerts - alert: HighErrorRate expr: increase(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) > 5 for: 2m severity: critical summary: "High 5xx error rate detected" description: "More than 5 server errors in the last 5 minutes." to Slack, email, or PagerDuty via Alertmanager: Your team wakes up before customers do.Define thresholds on latency panels (e.g. p95 > 500 ms)Trigger notifications to Microsoft Teams, OpsGenie, etc.Blend Sentry error counts with Prometheus metricsOverlay user‑flow drop‑offs (GA) on performance graphsBuild “service health” views for on‑call engineers6. Putting It All TogetherBy closing the loop — from zero visibility to full‑stack observability — you ensure your application is resilient and your team is empowered to act. your custom metrics (database pool usage, cache hit rates) on‑call runbooks via alert annotations logs, traces (Jaeger), and metrics for distributed tracing dashboards with stakeholders for data‑driven decision makingWith this foundation, you’ve transformed raw data into actionable insights — making performance, reliability, and user satisfaction a continuous, measurable journey.Enjoyed this guide? If you try these steps in your own project, drop a comment or share your Grafana dashboards!

item.title: From Monolith to Microservices: A Step-by-Step Guide for Seamless Transition
item.author: Anton Baksheiev
item.content:encoded: How to Seamlessly Shift from a Monolithic Architecture to Independent MicroservicesBreaking a into can be a complex process that requires careful planning, execution, and often a cultural shift within the organization. The goal is to split the monolith into smaller, independently deployable, and scalable services, each responsible for a specific business functionality. Here’s a general step-by-step approach to help you transition from a monolithic system to microservices:1. Understand the MonolithMap the current architecture: Before breaking anything, thoroughly understand how the monolithic system is structured. Identify major modules, dependencies, and components that interact with each other.Identify critical business domains: Look for different business functionalities that could form the basis for separate microservices. These might be modules like , , , etc.2. Define Clear Boundaries (Domain-Driven Design)Domain-driven design (DDD): Start by defining service boundaries based on business domains. This is one of the key principles in microservices. By breaking the monolith into services that represent , you ensure that each service is cohesive and responsible for a well-defined task.: Ensure that the boundaries for each microservice are clearly defined, meaning each service manages its own data and logic.3. Start with the Core ServicesExtract one service at a time: It’s best to by picking one of the least complex, self-contained components (such as user management or product catalog) to migrate first.: If your monolith uses a single database, start by splitting the database schema or transitioning to multiple databases — one for each microservice. Each microservice should manage its own database to avoid tight coupling.: Microservices should communicate via well-defined APIs, typically over or . You’ll need to expose public endpoints for the service you are migrating.4. Use a Strangler Fig Pattern: The is an incremental approach where the old monolith and new microservices coexist. Over time, you “strangle” the monolith by replacing parts of it with microservices.: You might set up a to route traffic to the appropriate microservice and legacy system, allowing you to migrate features gradually.: During the migration, the monolith may still be running, but new features are developed as microservices.5. Ensure Service CommunicationSynchronous communication: Microservices can communicate with each other using , , or for synchronous calls.Asynchronous communication: For better decoupling and scalability, use message queues or event streaming platforms (e.g., , ) for asynchronous communication. This is useful for handling events and background tasks like , , etc.6. Address Data Management and Consistency: In a monolithic architecture, you typically have a single database, but in microservices, each service often manages its own database. This introduces challenges with data consistency, so you might need to implement patterns using event-driven approaches.: For managing across multiple services, you can implement the . This helps coordinate long-running transactions, ensuring that they’re completed or compensated.7. Build Independent Deployment Pipelines: Each microservice should have its own for continuous integration and delivery. This ensures each service can be developed, tested, and deployed independently, without affecting others.: Use to package each microservice into a container, and or another orchestration tool to manage service deployment, scaling, and networking.8. Handle Authentication and AuthorizationCentralized authentication: If your monolith uses a central authentication system, consider migrating to an authentication service like or tokens for each service. Implement a or centralized identity management to maintain security across services.: Implement an to manage cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, and routing requests to the correct microservice.9. Monitor and Scale Services Independently: Implement centralized monitoring and logging (using tools like , , , or ) for all microservices. This is crucial to identify and troubleshoot issues quickly as the system scales.: Each microservice should be independently scalable based on demand. This allows your system to better handle increased load and optimize resource usage.Unit and integration tests: Microservices require thorough testing at both the and levels. Each service should be independently tested, and their interactions should also be tested.: Conduct to ensure that the system works as expected when the microservices communicate with each other.11. Decommission the MonolithOnce the migration is complete and all critical business functionalities have been moved to microservices, you can gradually decommission the monolith.Make sure to monitor the system closely during this phase to address any unforeseen issues that may arise as legacy features are removed.Challenges in the Process:: As you migrate, managing data consistency across microservices can become complex. Tools like or CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) may help.: Managing many services can increase operational complexity, which is why strong tooling (like Kubernetes, CI/CD, and monitoring) is essential.: Moving to microservices often involves adopting a DevOps culture, where teams work more independently and take ownership of specific services.Migrating from a monolithic architecture to microservices is a complex yet rewarding process that requires careful planning, clear boundaries, and incremental changes. By understanding the current monolith and identifying key business domains, you lay the foundation for a seamless transition. Domain-Driven Design (DDD) helps define clear service boundaries, ensuring each microservice is cohesive and responsible for its own data.Starting with core services allows you to manage the complexity step by step, using patterns like the Strangler Fig for a gradual, controlled migration. Ensuring robust communication between services, both synchronous and asynchronous, is essential for smooth operation in a distributed environment.Addressing challenges such as data consistency and implementing efficient service communication mechanisms, like event-driven patterns and APIs, will help maintain reliability and scalability. Moreover, building independent deployment pipelines and ensuring thorough testing at all stages ensures that each microservice can evolve independently without disrupting the overall system.The shift to microservices requires a cultural change, as teams take ownership of specific services and embrace a DevOps mindset. Monitoring, scaling independently, and decommissioning the monolith gradually are key aspects to keep the system running smoothly during and after the transition.While the process brings increased complexity and challenges, the long-term benefits, such as scalability, flexibility, and faster development cycles, make it a worthwhile endeavor. By taking a structured and careful approach, businesses can unlock the full potential of microservices and create a more resilient and efficient architecture.Thank you for reading! I hope you found this article helpful. If you have any questions, suggestions, or would like to discuss further, feel free to connect with me on . If you have any tips, experiences, or thoughts to add, I’d love to hear them in the comments below. Let’s keep the conversation going!

item.title: Kubernetes v1.33: Updates to Container Lifecycle
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes v1.33 introduces a few updates to the lifecycle of containers. The Sleep action for container lifecycle hooks now supports a zero sleep duration (feature enabled by default). There is also alpha support for customizing the stop signal sent to containers when they are being terminated.This blog post goes into the details of these new aspects of the container lifecycle, and how you can use them.Zero value for Sleep actionKubernetes v1.29 introduced the action for container PreStop and PostStart Lifecycle hooks. The Sleep action lets your containers pause for a specified duration after the container is started or before it is terminated. This was needed to provide a straightforward way to manage graceful shutdowns. Before the Sleep action, folks used to run the command using the exec action in their container lifecycle hooks. If you wanted to do this you'd need to have the binary for the command in your container image. This is difficult if you're using third party images.The sleep action when it was added initially didn't have support for a sleep duration of zero seconds. The which the Sleep action uses under the hood supports a duration of zero seconds. Using a negative or a zero value for the sleep returns immediately, resulting in a no-op. We wanted the same behaviour with the sleep action. This support for the zero duration was later added in v1.32, with the PodLifecycleSleepActionAllowZero feature gate.The PodLifecycleSleepActionAllowZero feature gate has graduated to beta in v1.33, and is now enabled by default. The original Sleep action for and hooks is been enabled by default, starting from Kubernetes v1.30. With a cluster running Kubernetes v1.33, you are able to set a zero duration for sleep lifecycle hooks. For a cluster with default configuration, you don't need to enable any feature gate to make that possible.Container runtimes such as containerd and CRI-O honor a instruction in the container image definition. This can be used to specify a custom stop signal that the runtime will used to terminate containers based on that image. Stop signal configuration was not originally part of the Pod API in Kubernetes. Until Kubernetes v1.33, the only way to override the stop signal for containers was by rebuilding your container image with the new custom stop signal (for example, specifying in a or ).The feature gate which is newly added in Kubernetes v1.33 adds stop signals to the Kubernetes API. This allows users to specify a custom stop signal in the container spec. Stop signals are added to the API as a new lifecycle along with the existing PreStop and PostStart lifecycle handlers. In order to use this feature, we expect the Pod to have the operating system specified with . This is enforced so that we can cross-validate the stop signal against the operating system and make sure that the containers in the Pod are created with a valid stop signal for the operating system the Pod is being scheduled to. For Pods scheduled on Windows nodes, only and are allowed as valid stop signals. Find the full list of signals supported in Linux nodes here.If a container has a custom stop signal defined in its lifecycle, the container runtime would use the signal defined in the lifecycle to kill the container, given that the container runtime also supports custom stop signals. If there is no custom stop signal defined in the container lifecycle, the runtime would fallback to the stop signal defined in the container image. If there is no stop signal defined in the container image, the default stop signal of the runtime would be used. The default signal is for both containerd and CRI-O.For the feature to work as intended, both the versions of Kubernetes and the container runtime should support container stop signals. The changes to the Kuberentes API and kubelet are available in alpha stage from v1.33, which can be enabled with the feature gate. The container runtime implementations for containerd and CRI-O are still a work in progress and will be rolled out soon.Using container stop signalsTo enable this feature, you need to turn on the feature gate in both the kube-apiserver and the kubelet. Once you have nodes where the feature gate is turned on, you can create Pods with a StopSignal lifecycle and a valid OS name like so:Do note that the signal in this example can only be used if the container's Pod is scheduled to a Linux node. Hence we need to specify as to be able to use the signal. You will only be able to configure and signals if the Pod is being scheduled to a Windows node. You cannot specify a containers[*].lifecycle.stopSignal if the field is nil or unset either.This feature is driven by the SIG Node. If you are interested in helping develop this feature, sharing feedback, or participating in any other ongoing SIG Node projects, please reach out to us!You can reach SIG Node by several means:You can also contact me directly:GitHub: @sreeram-venkiteshSlack: @sreeram.venkitesh

item.title: How to enhance your application resiliency using Amazon Q Developer
item.author: Dr. Rahul Sharad Gaikwad
item.content:encoded: “Everything fails, all the time” – Werner Vogels, Amazon.com CTOIn today’s digital landscape, designing applications with resilience in mind is crucial. Resiliency is the ability of applications to handle failures gracefully, adapt to changing conditions, and recover swiftly from disruptions. By integrating resilience into your application architecture, you can minimize downtime, mitigate the impact of failures, and ensure continuous availability and performance for end-users.Amazon Q Developer, a generative AI-powered assistant for software development lifecycle (SDLC), helps design resilient architectures and enhance application availability. It recommends best practices, analyzes code, and identifies potential failure points, serving as an expert companion to strengthen application architecture and boost system availability through the following key resiliency practices.Resilient design pattern recommendations: Access tailored design patterns like distributed systems, microservices, and serverless architectures. Amazon Q offers recommendations across redundancy, robust failovers, and circuit breakers to boost resilience in your environment.Disaster Recovery planning: Amazon Q offers expert guidance on comprehensive disaster recovery (DR), including efficient backups, systematic restorations, strategic data replication, and seamless failovers to ensure rapid recovery from disruptions with minimal impact.Customized Resiliency testing frameworks: Create custom templates to simulate diverse failure scenarios, such as network degradation and infrastructure outages. This streamlines thorough resilience verification across your systems. Use Amazon Q to conduct comprehensive Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) identifying infrastructure vulnerabilities and assessing their impact. Amazon Q then ranks these issues by severity, enabling you to prioritize and address the most critical risks to protect your production environment.In the following sections, we will demonstrate how Amazon Q improves the resiliency of a foundational application architecture.To begin using Amazon Q, the following are required:We have a three-tier web application shown below that is running on AWS in a single Availability Zone (AZ). The architecture consists of hosted on Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) cluster with two Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) nodes in a single-AZ and the uses Amazon Relational Database Service (Amazon RDS) instance deployed in single-AZ configuration. The architecture is functional but has several limitations. It poses a single point of failure and offers limited application availability with no fault tolerance. High response times may occur because there is no caching layer in front of the database. Additionally, the lack of auto-scaling can lead to resource contention.Basic Application OverviewEnhance Application ResiliencyLet’s explore how Amazon Q helps incorporate resiliency best practices that enhance system availability in our basic application architecture.Resilient architecture recommendationsThe initial architecture faced challenges with reliability, performance and scalability, largely due to its single-point of failure and lacked redundancy. To address this, we described the existing application design and its challenges to Amazon Q using a natural language prompt to seek resiliency recommendations.Prompt for improving the architecture design:I have manually setup an application that runs within an EKS cluster on two EC2 nodes in single AZ. My application is not highly available and scalable. It talks to an RDS database which is single AZ. However, there is high response times from database. Provide me only the recommendations to re-design this application architecture at each layer that will addresses all these issues.Amazon Q offering resiliency architecture recommendationsAmazon Q analyzed the provided context and recommended improvements such as introducing Multi-AZ deployments for high availability, adding auto-scaling groups for elasticity, and incorporating caching layers to enhance performance. These targeted recommendations helped redesign the architecture to be more resilient and scalable, directly addressing the initial shortcomings.Disaster Recovery (DR) recommendations to improve the architectureTo further enhance resiliency, we prompted Amazon Q for disaster recovery (DR) recommendations. We asked for guidance aligned with the AWS Well-Architected Framework. This built upon the previously improved architecture design.Prompt for recommendations on Disaster Recovery (DR) and architecture based on RTO/RPOBased on the above improvements on AWS architecture design, share recommendations for Disaster Recovery (DR) based on AWS Well Architected FrameworkOptionally, we can use advanced prompts like the below with additional context:Please provide a recommendations to redesign my application that is running on an EKS cluster with two EC2 nodes and a single-AZ RDS database, addressing high database latency, low availability, and scalability issues. Suggest improvements across all architectural layers including presentation tier, application tier and data tier to enhance performance, resiliency, and scalability. Also, recommend DR strategies aligned with the AWS Well-Architected Framework focusing on resilience, data protection, and recovery.Amazon Q tailoring recommendations based on business requirements using AWS Well-Architected FrameworkPrepare DR strategy based on RTO and RPO requirements:Diving further, asking for a specific disaster recovery strategy that meets the application RTO requirements of 2 hours and RPO requirements of 30 minutes.Prompt for DR strategy based on RTO/RPO valuesWhich DR strategy should I use if my RTO is less than 2 hours and RPO is less than 30 minutes?Amazon Q recommending disaster recovery strategyAmazon Q recommended a Pilot light approach, detailing the setup and components needed to achieve the specified disaster recovery objectives.Define resiliency testing workflow, identify key metrics and toolsAs we incorporate resiliency best practices into the application architecture, its is important to employ a resiliency test workflow to ensure application’s resiliency requirements are met. To do this, we are asking for guidance to define an end-to-end resiliency testing process workflow. We also want to identify the key metrics and tools needed to test the resilience of each AWS service involved in the architecture.Prompt for defining the resiliency testing workflow:Define the end-to-end resiliency testing process workflow. Also, identify the key metrics and tools that should be used to test the resilience of each AWS service involved in the improved architecture design.Amazon Q offering resiliency testing best practices and toolsAmazon Q offers a step-by-step approach to define resiliency testing experiments and prepare the environment for testing.Failure mode evaluation to prioritize resiliency testsFailure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) can further assist with designing the resiliency tests. It is a proactive method to identify potential failures in processes or systems, assess their impact, and prioritize critical issues. It evaluates failure modes across hardware, software, human factors, and external events, enabling teams to develop strategies for prevention, detection, and mitigation, ultimately enhancing system resilience.Leveraging Amazon Q, we requested a comprehensive FMEA report that includes components, cause, effect and their respective Risk Priority Numbers (RPN). RPNs are calculated by multiplying three key factors: Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D). It helps organizations understand and prioritize which risks to address first.Prompt for designing the FMEA template and scoring:Create the FMEA in tabular format with scoring for improved architecture design above keeping in mind the RTO/RPO values and provide the steps for execution as well.Amazon Q assisting with systematic risk assessment and FMEA reportAmazon Q intelligently incorporated previously defined RTO and RPO requirements to identify critical failure scenarios and calculated RPN for each potential incident.Enhanced Architecture Implementing Resiliency Best PracticesAfter identifying the key pain points in our original architecture such as single points of failure, limited scalability, and lack of automated recovery, we leveraged Amazon Q to analyze our architecture to get targeted recommendations to elevate the resiliency. By describing our requirements and challenges to Amazon Q, we received actionable guidance on AWS best practices and service configurations, which we then implemented to transform our infrastructure for high resilience and availability.Resilient Application ArchitectureThe original was running in a single Availability Zone without auto-scaling, leading to potential downtime and performance bottlenecks. Amazon Q recommended distributing Amazon EKS worker nodes across multiple Availability Zones and enabling the Cluster Autoscaler to dynamically adjust node capacity based on traffic patterns. Additionally, it suggested implementing horizontal pod autoscaling within Amazon EKS to automatically scale application resources according to CPU utilization and custom metrics. Following these recommendations, we deployed Amazon EKS worker nodes across three Availability Zones, configured Cluster Autoscaler and horizontal pod autoscaling, and integrated an Application Load Balancer, to intelligently distribute incoming traffic. These changes significantly improved scalability, fault tolerance, and performance.The initially relied on a single-instance Amazon RDS deployment, which posed a risk of downtime and limited read performance. Upon review, Amazon Q advised implementing a Multi-AZ Amazon RDS configuration to enable automated failover and improve availability. It also recommended deploying read replicas to offload read-heavy workloads and enhance performance. Furthermore, Amazon Q suggested adding a Multi-AZ Amazon ElastiCache for Redis to reduce database load and speed up data access. We incorporated these recommendations, resulting in a more resilient and performant data layer capable of handling failover scenarios and scaling read operations efficiently.The lacked an optimized content delivery mechanism and comprehensive security controls. Amazon Q recommended integrating Amazon CloudFront as a content delivery network to accelerate the delivery of static content and reduce load on application servers. It also suggested deploying AWS WAF to protect against common web exploits. To improve operational visibility, Amazon Q emphasized the importance of comprehensive monitoring using Amazon CloudWatch, combining logs, metrics, and traces for rapid issue detection and resolution. Implementing these recommendations enhanced both the performance and security posture of the presentation layer.Amazon Q Developer transforms how teams build resilient applications by serving as your expert companion throughout the development journey. Its guidance helps create systems that excel in resilience, scalability, and availability—critical factors for today’s demanding digital landscape. Amazon Q goes beyond theoretical advice by providing practical, step-by-step implementation guidance. In the above, we’ve witnessed how Amazon Q’s expertise can transform basic architectures into robust, failure-resistant systems. Its recommendations such as Multi-AZ redundancy, elastic scaling, strategic caching, and proactive resilience testing create applications that maintain performance and availability even during significant disruptions.Ready to strengthen your applications against unexpected challenges? Harness Amazon Q’s capabilities to create resilient infrastructure that consistently delivers for your customers, regardless of conditions. Unlock the full potential of your AWS infrastructure and deliver uninterrupted service to your customers, today. To learn more about Amazon Q refer to the documentation.

item.title: Survey: Software Quality Continues to Be Sacrificed in the Name of Speed
item.author: Mike Vizard
